From my early years in college, I became addicted to biological science, a field with so many important yet unanswered questions. I was fascinated, for example, by questions such as what defines a cell's life or death, which molecules make the decisions to live or die, and how these decisions are precisely executed. Given the enormous complexity of biological systems, these problems are beyond our scope of handling if we are not aided by the power of computers. This leads to my interest in bioinformatics.

My first impression about the use of computers in biology came with my work at DuPont Merck two years ago. To elucidate the mechanism by which rabbit thrombin is activated, I sequenced the rabbit prothrombin cDNA and compared its primary protein structure with several related proteins using three bioinformatics programs. We found that the difference at the putative cleavage sites within prothrombin may contribute to its unusual susceptibility to auto-cleavage, which is consistent with the previous observation. Without the help of bioinformatics, we would have had to perform more tedious biochemical experiments. Recently, my work with Molecular Sciences Institute has provided me with a better appreciation about what advantages informatics can bring to our biological scientists. Using powerful tools such as the yeast two-hybrid system, we can generate a wealth of interaction data. It is protein-protein interactions that determine many important biological reactions involved in signal transduction, transcriptional and translational regulation, and ultimately, cellular decision making. With the help of relational databases, we can discover networks of interacting proteins that would have not been detected by traditional biochemical methods.

I am excited about the powerful use of computer in biology research and the possibility of exploring problems that are difficult to tackle by the traditional approaches. To accomplish my goals, I realize that I am in great need of formal and systematic training in informatics, a better understanding of how to apply statistics in data analysis, and the fundamentals of biological database design. With adequate training, I hope to attack some of the questions in which I am interested. For example, with the availability of raw protein interaction and molecular genetic data, can we design efficient databases to collect the data? Can we extract useful information from these databases to facilitate the simulation of some of the interesting and important pathways such as those involved in cell cycle regulation and apoptotic pathway? If a realistic simulation is possible, can we introduce experimental variables (eg, different concentrations of few major regulatory proteins) and ask the computer to generate new hypotheses that can be readily tested experimentally? All in all, I believe a systematic training in bioinformatics would provide me with great flexibility and adequate knowledge base for my future career.
